data.title: Ulta Beauty updates 2025 outlook with net sales guidance of $11.5B–$11.7B amid strong Q1 execution and new brand launches
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ulta Beauty (ULTA) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Kecia Steelman, President & CEO, highlighted that "Fiscal 2025 is off to an encouraging start, with the Ulta Beauty team delivering better than planned financial performance." She reported net sales increased 4.5% to $2.8 billion, operating profit at 14.1% of sales, and diluted EPS of $6.70. Steelman emphasized market share gains, growth in member engagement, and strong in-store performance, stating "positive comp sales growth in-stores for the first-time in more than a year."
  • Steelman noted fragrance as the strongest-performing category, with double-digit growth led by new and exclusive launches such as XO Khloe by Khloe Kardashian and Noise, and newness from Valentino and Billie Eilish. Skincare and wellness delivered high-single-digit growth, while haircare was flat and makeup comp sales decreased slightly. She also pointed to successful marketing campaigns and a record 45 million active loyalty members, up 3% year-over-year.
  • The CEO announced the expansion of brand launches with 19 new brands in the quarter and updated on digital investments, including new app features and automation. Steelman confirmed international expansion plans, targeting store openings in Mexico City, Kuwait City, and Dubai later this year, and a new online marketplace initiative set to launch in the second half.
  • Operationally, Steelman cited AI and machine learning initiatives to drive supply chain efficiencies and introduced Lauren Brindley as the incoming Chief Merchandising and Digital Officer, effective June 3rd.
  • Paula Oyibo, CFO, stated: "The Ulta Beauty team delivered strong performance, reflecting better than expected growth from comparable sales, favorable shrink results, and lower than planned investment spend."

Outlook

  • Management updated its annual guidance, projecting net sales between $11.5 billion and $11.7 billion with comparable sales growth in the range of flat to up 1.5%. Diluted EPS is now expected to be between $22.65 and $23.20 per share. Operating margin is forecast between 11.7% and 11.8% of sales.
  • CFO Oyibo stated, "We believe it is prudent to take a cautious approach to our guidance for fiscal 2025," citing ongoing consumer pressures and global trade uncertainty, particularly in the second half of the year. The company expects comp growth in the second half to be in the range of down low-single digits to up modestly.

Financial Results

  • Net sales rose 4.5% to $2.8 billion. Comparable sales increased 2.9%, driven by a 2.3% increase in average ticket and a 0.6% increase in transactions.
  • Other revenue decreased $4 million to $56 million, primarily due to lower loyalty point redemptions and credit card income.
  • Gross margin decreased 10 basis points to 39.1%. SG&A increased 6.7% to $711 million, and as a percentage of sales, rose 50 basis points to 24.9%, mainly due to higher store payroll and benefits.
  • Diluted EPS increased 3.6% to $6.70. Inventory rose 11.3% to $2.1 billion, reflecting support for new brands and growth categories, and capital expenditures were $79 million. The company returned $359 million to shareholders through share repurchases.

Q&A

  • Rupesh Parikh, Oppenheimer: Asked about the Ulta Beauty Unleashed plan's progress and surprises. Steelman responded that the quarter "reflects our collective effort of the team and our commitment to really improving our overall process," calling out improved in-store execution, marketing, and product assortment.
  • Olivia Tong, Raymond James: Queried the cautious full-year outlook and promotional/price strategy. Oyibo explained the outlook reflects Q1 strength but more uncertainty in the second half, emphasizing a rational promotional environment and ongoing optimization efforts.
  • Susan Anderson, Canaccord Genuity: Sought color on newness and innovation. Steelman noted a "nice cadence of newness and exclusivity within the newness and it's balanced across the portfolio."
  • Korinne Wolfmeyer, Piper Sandler: Asked about improvements in demand and competitive intensity. Steelman stated the Ulta Beauty Unleashed plan helped drive share gains and balanced performance across categories.
  • Christopher Horvers, JPMorgan: Probed execution vs. competitive headwinds and ERP disruption. Steelman highlighted focus on controllable factors, improved in-stocks, and better marketing.
  • Other analysts inquired about ticket/transactions, marketing strategy, brand curation, personalization, UB Media, and e-commerce mix; management cited continued investment in digital, exclusive brands, and data-driven personalization.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism but frequently pressed management on the sustainability of growth, competitive dynamics, and the rationale behind a conservative outlook despite strong Q1 results. Tone was neutral with occasional skepticism about the macro environment and second-half guidance.
  • Management maintained a confident but measured tone, especially in prepared remarks, frequently referring to the need for prudence and agility. During Q&A, Steelman and Oyibo reiterated the rationale for conservative guidance, often emphasizing uncertainty and the importance of controlling what can be controlled.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management exhibited slightly increased confidence, though management remained cautious about second-half risks and macro pressures.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw improvement in both net sales growth and comp sales, as well as positive in-store comps, compared to the previous quarter's challenges with market share and execution.
  • Guidance was raised at the upper end for net sales and EPS but remains cautious due to increased uncertainty. The focus on new brand launches and digital enhancements intensified this quarter.
  • Analysts continued to probe on competitive threats, promotional strategies, and innovation, as in the prior quarter, but questions about the sustainability of newness and macro pressures took on increased importance.
  • Management’s tone reflected more optimism about operational execution and the impact of strategic initiatives, whereas last quarter focused more on foundational changes and transition plans.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited consumer wallet pressures, global trade uncertainty, and evolving tariffs as ongoing risks.
  • Steelman indicated the team is "working in close partnership with our brands to mitigate potential impacts of higher tariffs."
  • Oyibo referenced the need for a "cautious approach" to guidance, given macroeconomic volatility and potential for increased pressure in the second half.
  • Analysts raised concerns about competitive intensity, promotional environment, and the ability to sustain momentum amid external headwinds.

Final Takeaway

Ulta Beauty management emphasized a solid start to fiscal 2025 with strong financial performance, positive in-store trends, and market share gains. The company is advancing strategic investments in new brands, digital capabilities, and international expansion, while maintaining a cautious outlook for the remainder of the year due to persistent macroeconomic uncertainty and evolving global trade dynamics. Management reinforced confidence in the Ulta Beauty Unleashed plan and its ability to drive sustainable long-term growth, highlighting continued agility and operational discipline as key to navigating the current environment.

data.title: Banco Macro outlines revised 2025 guidance with 60% loan growth and ROE target cut to 8–10% amid changing market conditions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Banco Macro S.A. (BMA) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Jorge Scarinci, CFO, reported that "Banco Macro's net income totaled ARS45.7 billion" for the first quarter, noting this was "59%, or ARS65.3 billion, lower than the fourth quarter of 2024" due to "lower net income from financial assets and liabilities at fair value through profit or loss and the bigger loss related to the result from the net monetary position, higher inflation was observed in the quarter, which was partially offset by higher other operating income and lower employee benefits and administrative expenses."
  • The annualized return on average equity stood at 3.8%, and the accumulated annualized return on average assets at 1.2%.
  • Net operating income before general and administrative expenses was ARS801 billion, down 9% quarter-on-quarter, with the decrease mainly attributed to lower income from interest on government securities.
  • Provision for loan losses reached ARS66 billion, 62% higher than the previous quarter, which Scarinci attributed to "the loan growth experienced in the quarter."
  • The bank's total financing reached ARS7.7 trillion, a 22% sequential increase, with private sector loans also up 22%. Banco Macro's market share over private sector loans as of March 2025 was 9.5%.
  • Total deposits rose 5% quarter-on-quarter to ARS9.6 trillion, with private sector deposits up 4%. Market share of private sector deposits as of March 2025 reached 7.8%.
  • Scarinci stated, "We continue showing a solid financial position. We keep a well optimized deposit base. Asset quality remained under control and closely monitored."

Outlook

  • Scarinci indicated loan growth guidance remains at "60% loan growth in real terms for 2025." Deposit growth guidance was raised to "a real growth of 45%" from the previously communicated 30%.
  • The capital ratio forecast for year-end 2025 was lifted to "28%, 29%."
  • The return on equity (ROE) target was revised lower to "between 8% to 10% ROE for 2025," down from the previous range of 12–15%. Scarinci cautioned, "this is going to be or could be affected by the evolution of bond prices."
  • Management stated the current focus is on organic growth, but Scarinci added, "we are positive that in the next couple of years or three years, the number of banks in Argentina might shrink. And we are going to be on alert and trying to analyze any acquisition target that should appear in the market."

Financial Results

  • Net interest income for the quarter was ARS579.2 billion, marginally higher than the prior quarter. Interest income totaled ARS866.7 billion, down 3% sequentially.
  • Income from interest on loans reached ARS592.3 billion, up 9% from the previous quarter, driven by an 18% increase in the average volume of private sector loans, partially offset by a decrease in the average lending rate.
  • Fee income was ARS169.8 billion, relatively stable compared to the previous quarter.
  • Net income from financial assets and liabilities at fair value was ARS66.4 billion, a 55% decrease from the prior quarter.
  • Administrative expenses plus employee benefits totaled ARS257 billion, a 10% decrease quarter-on-quarter.
  • The efficiency ratio improved slightly to 38.2% from 39.4% in the previous quarter.
  • Non-performing total financial ratio reached 1.44%, with a coverage ratio of 163.34%.
  • The bank's excess capital was ARS3.2 trillion, representing a capital adequacy ratio of 34.3% and a Tier 1 ratio of 33.6%.

Q&A

  • Brian Flores, Citi: Asked for an update on guidance and capital strategy. Scarinci responded that the loan growth and capital ratio forecasts are maintained or marginally increased, while the ROE target is now lower at 8–10%. He added that the bank is prioritizing organic growth and monitoring for potential M&A opportunities.
  • Flores followed up on deposit growth drivers. Scarinci explained, "the first quarter, in terms of volume growth, both in loans and deposit, but more in deposits were slightly ahead of expectations... we are seeing maybe dollar deposit growth slightly stronger than what we had expected."
  • Ernesto Gabilondo, Bank of America: Asked about macro expectations, operating expenses, and capital deployment. Scarinci projected "real growth for this year of 5%" in GDP, "30% area inflation in 2025," and expects operating expenses to move "close to inflation rate... maybe 1 percentage point or 2 percentage points below." On capital, he repeated that while M&A is possible, "we are not analyzing anything for the moment."
  • Carlos Gomez-Lopez, HSBC: Inquired about the bond portfolio and CEO introduction. Scarinci said the bank is comfortable with high exposure to inflation-linked instruments and prefers available-for-sale classification to allow flexibility. He noted, "For the moment, we believe that is the best way to hedge the equity of the bank."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts frequently pressed for details on guidance revisions, capital deployment, and deposit strategies, reflecting a slightly negative tone on the reduced ROE outlook and increased provisions.
  • Management's responses were measured, with a focus on prudence and adaptability. Scarinci used language such as "We believe..." and "we are going to be on alert," indicating a cautious but prepared stance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from confident projections of recovery to a more tempered outlook, particularly regarding profitability.
  • Analyst tone was more skeptical this quarter, especially regarding the lower ROE guidance and the implications for growth and capital use.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Loan growth guidance remained at 60%, but deposit growth guidance increased from 30% to 45%.
  • The capital adequacy ratio outlook was raised slightly, while the ROE target was cut from 12–15% to 8–10%, reflecting more cautious profit expectations.
  • Management's tone was more guarded, highlighting risks around bond prices and macroeconomic volatility.
  • Analysts focused more intently on capital efficiency, organic versus inorganic growth, and the sustainability of margins.
  • Asset quality saw a minor deterioration in consumer loans, while the coverage ratio improved.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "higher inflation was observed in the quarter" and "bigger loss related to the result from the net monetary position."
  • Provision for loan losses increased 62% quarter-on-quarter, attributed to rapid loan growth.
  • Management highlighted that future ROE is sensitive to bond price fluctuations.
  • Analysts questioned the sustainability of deposit and loan growth targets amid competitive pressures and regulatory uncertainty.

Final Takeaway

Banco Macro's first quarter of 2025 showed robust loan and deposit growth, but profitability expectations have been tempered with a revised lower ROE outlook of 8–10% for the year. While the bank maintains a strong capital position and continues to focus on organic growth, management is prepared to consider M&A opportunities should they arise. The operating environment remains challenging due to inflation and volatile bond prices, and management is closely monitoring asset quality and capital deployment to navigate these conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Dell raises FY2026 EPS guidance to $9.40 with $15B+ AI server shipment target, driven by record AI demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Dell Technologies (DELL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Jeff Clarke, Vice Chairman and COO, reported "revenue reached $23.4 billion, up 5%, driven by growth across all of our core markets. ISG and CSG were up 8%. Earnings per share increased by 17% to $1.55, growing 3 times faster than revenue. This resulted in a record cash generation for the first quarter and shareholder returns exceeding $2 billion." Clarke emphasized "unprecedented demand for our AI-optimized servers," detailing $12.1 billion in AI server orders and a backlog of $14.4 billion. He highlighted strong sequential growth in enterprise AI customers and expansion across verticals.
  • Clarke noted Dell's innovation pace, "designing bespoke custom solutions," and pointed to partnerships with NVIDIA, AMD, HuggingFace, Meta, Mistral, and Google. He announced the launch of several new AI and server products, including the PowerCool platform and Dell AI Data Platform, and highlighted exclusive partnerships such as bringing Gemini on-prem with Google and collaboration with Cohere.
  • Clarke stated, "We are leading in AI and pushing the boundaries of innovation. We had over $12 billion in AI orders this quarter alone, which will drive significant revenue growth and EPS."
  • Yvonne McGill, CFO, stated, "Our total revenue was up 5%, to $23.4 billion. Our combined ISG and CSG business grew 8%. Gross margin was $5.1 billion, or 21.6% of revenue. This is down 80 basis points due to a more competitive pricing environment, predominantly in CSG, and geographical mix within traditional servers." McGill added, "We delivered a 10% increase to $1.7 billion, or 7.1% of revenue. Q1 net income was up 13% to $1.1 billion, primarily driven by stronger operating income."

Outlook

  • McGill provided Q2 guidance: "We expect Q2 revenue to be between $28.5 billion and $29.5 billion, up 16% at the midpoint of $29 billion. ISG and CSG combined are expected to grow 19% at the midpoint, with ISG growing significantly and CSG up low-to-mid single digits." She continued, "We expect our diluted non-GAAP EPS is expected to be $2.25, plus or minus $0.10, up 15% at the midpoint."
  • For the full year, McGill reiterated, "we are reiterating our full year revenue guidance and expect FY 2026 revenue to be between $101 billion and $105 billion, with a midpoint of $103 billion, up 8%. We expect ISG to grow high-teens, driven by over $15 billion in AI server shipments and continued growth in traditional server and storage. And we expect CSG to grow low-to-mid single digits."
  • McGill announced, "We are increasing our diluted non-GAAP EPS guidance to $9.40, plus or minus $0.25, up 15% at the midpoint."

Financial Results

  • Dell reported Q1 revenue of $23.4 billion; ISG and CSG grew 8%. Gross margin was $5.1 billion, operating income was $1.7 billion, and net income was $1.1 billion. Diluted EPS was $1.55.
  • ISG revenue was $10.3 billion, up 12%. Servers and networking revenue was a Q1 record of $6.3 billion, up 16%. Storage revenue was up 6% to $4.0 billion. ISG operating income was $1.0 billion, up 36%.
  • CSG revenue was $12.5 billion, up 5%. Commercial revenue was $11.0 billion, up 9%; Consumer revenue was $1.5 billion, down 19%. CSG operating income was $0.7 billion.
  • Record Q1 cash flow from operations totaled $2.8 billion. Dell ended the quarter with $9.3 billion in cash and investments, returning $2.4 billion to shareholders via repurchases and dividends.

Q&A

  • Amit Daryanani, Evercore: Asked about the fiscal 2026 AI server revenue target and market outlook. Clarke responded, "$4.1 billion of orders in Q1, more than all of last year, we're off to a good start...the backlog is $15.4 billion on top of that $12.1 billion of orders...we're on the plus side of $15 billion and feel pretty good about it."
  • Ben Reitzes, Melius Research: Queried about second-half guidance and lumpiness in AI server shipments. Clarke said, "We're closer to $9 billion in the first half...the five-quarter pipeline continues to grow and grow significantly."
  • Erik Woodring, Morgan Stanley: Sought details on storage and services attach rates with AI servers. Clarke stated, "We've made significant improvement in our deployment and installation services attach...we are not satisfied with the attach today. I think that is all upside and opportunity."
  • Wamsi Mohan, Bank of America: Asked about tariffs and AI server operating margins. Clarke replied, "Our guidance for the year and for Q2 includes everything that we know about tariffs as of today...We did not make any price moves in the quarter."
  • Michael Ng, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about the AI server profit outlook and order characteristics. McGill answered, "We are expecting rates to be down going forward...driven by lower profitability in traditional service and CSG. Storage margins are also improving nicely."
  • Samik Chatterjee, JPMorgan: Questioned on customer demand pull forward and macro caution. Clarke said, "I'm sure [pull forward] did happen. To what degree? I don't know...Month three slowed in weeks 10 through 12...We now have that reflected in our guidance in Q2."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive but probing tone, focusing on AI backlog, profitability of AI servers, and impacts of macro and competitive pressures, with frequent requests for clarification on guidance and margin trends.
  • Management showed confidence in both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing execution, pipeline growth, and the robustness of the AI opportunity. Clarke stated, "I am confident in our position and ability to execute. We are leading in AI and pushing the boundaries of innovation."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained assertive, but demonstrated increased caution around traditional server and CSG profitability, while highlighting upside in AI-driven operating income.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for FY 2026 revenue ($101-$105 billion) and $15 billion in AI server shipments was reiterated, consistent with the previous quarter, but EPS guidance increased to $9.40 from $9.30.
  • ISG revenue growth rate moderated from 22% in Q4 to 12% in Q1; CSG revenue growth accelerated from 1% in Q4 to 5% in Q1.
  • Gross margin decreased to 21.6% from 24.3%, attributed to a competitive pricing environment and geographical/server mix shifts.
  • Management continues to emphasize AI as the primary growth driver, with a record AI server backlog and pipeline. Analysts’ focus shifted more heavily toward AI order conversion, profitability, and traditional server softness.
  • Management’s tone remained confident but signaled increased vigilance regarding macro dynamics and competitive landscape in CSG and traditional servers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing variability and timing in AI demand and shipments, with Clarke observing "the inherent nonlinear nature of demand and associated shipments is likely to persist."
  • McGill noted "a more competitive pricing environment, predominantly in CSG, and geographical mix within traditional servers" as headwinds impacting gross margin.
  • Clarke mentioned a "slowdown in month three...in all three US businesses, commercial PCs, traditional servers, and storage," now reflected in Q2 guidance.
  • Analysts raised concerns about attach rates for storage and services with AI servers, tariff impacts, macro-driven demand caution, and competitive intensity in both CSG and ISG.

Final Takeaway

Dell Technologies delivered a strong Q1 2026, highlighted by record AI server demand and robust order pipeline, setting the stage for more than $15 billion in AI server shipments this fiscal year. While traditional server and CSG profitability face competitive and macro challenges, management raised EPS guidance and reiterated confidence in the company’s unique position to capitalize on accelerating AI infrastructure demand, supported by deep partnerships, innovation, and a resilient supply chain.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gap outlines $100M–$150M tariff mitigation strategy while reaffirming 1–2% net sales growth for 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Gap, Inc. (GAP) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Richard Dickson, President and CEO, emphasized, “The first quarter was another great quarter, during which we delivered what we said we were going to do, exceeding expectations across key financial metrics. We had positive comp sales for the fifth consecutive quarter, expanded both gross margin and operating margin, and gained market share for the ninth consecutive quarter.”
  • Dickson highlighted continued growth at Old Navy and Gap, with Old Navy comp sales up 3% and Gap up 5%. He noted, “Old Navy and Gap saw growth across all income cohorts, with Old Navy gaining share in both top and bottom cohorts and Gap gaining share in top and middle cohorts, showing our strategic intent is working.”
  • The CEO detailed sourcing diversification efforts: “China, as an example, used to be one of the top sourcing countries for our product. In 2024, it represented less than 10% of our sourcing. And exiting 2025, we now expect it to be less than 3%.”
  • On tariffs, Dickson stated, “We are working to develop plans to mitigate as much of the anticipated tariff impact as possible, taking actions in the short term without compromising the long-term integrity of our strategy.”
  • Katrina O'Connell, CFO, said, “In the first quarter, we once again exceeded financial expectations, demonstrating that the meaningful progress we've made on our strategic priorities is translating into strong results.”

Outlook

  • The company reaffirmed its fiscal 2025 outlook for net sales up 1% to 2% year-over-year, with operating income growth in the 8% to 10% range excluding any tariff impact.
  • O'Connell explained, “We are currently estimating a net impact of approximately $100 million to $150 million to fiscal 2025 operating margin based on current tariff policy.”
  • For Q2, net sales are expected to be roughly flat year-over-year, with gross margin expected to be similar to Q1 and SG&A planned to leverage slightly versus last year.
  • Management reiterated their intent to pursue $150 million in cost savings and efficiencies in 2025.

Financial Results

  • Net sales for Q1 were $3.5 billion, a 2% increase year-over-year, with comparable sales also up 2%.
  • Old Navy net sales reached $2 billion, up 3%, while Gap brand net sales were $724 million, up 5%.
  • Banana Republic net sales were $428 million (down 3%), and Athleta net sales were $308 million (down 6%).
  • Gross margin was 41.8%, a 60 basis point increase, and SG&A was $1.2 billion, flat year-over-year.
  • Operating margin reached 7.5%, up 140 basis points, and EPS was $0.51, a 24% increase versus last year.
  • The quarter ended with $2.2 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments.
  • Inventory levels were up 7% year-over-year due to earlier receipts and faster transit times.

Q&A

  • Alex Straton, Morgan Stanley, asked about further tariff mitigation potential. Dickson responded, “We already have strategies to mitigate over half of the anticipated impact of tariffs… we remain committed to achieving additional mitigation over time.” O'Connell added that the estimated net impact of tariffs is currently $100 million to $150 million, primarily weighted to the second half of the year.
  • Adrienne Yih, Barclays, inquired about pricing as part of mitigation. Dickson clarified, “Based on what we know today, we do not expect there to be meaningful price impacts to our consumer.”
  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan, questioned customer acquisition and brand consistency. Dickson explained, “New customer acquisition is increasing… collabs, while driving new traffic and new volume, 25% of that basket includes core product.” O’Connell affirmed the multiyear model for low to mid-single digit sales growth with high single digit operating margin growth.
  • Lorraine Hutchinson, Bank of America, asked about merchandise margin. O’Connell highlighted, “Underlying merch margins [were] relatively flat… margin expansion really coming from Rod.”
  • Dana Telsey, Telsey Group, focused on Banana Republic and Athleta as well as channel performance. Dickson said Banana Republic showed “great new proof points emerging,” while Athleta's performance remains choppy as the brand reset continues. Dickson also reported online business grew 6% and now represents 39% of total net sales.
  • Paul Lejuez, Citigroup, asked about comp drivers and AUR. O’Connell stated, “AUR was down modestly, but that was honestly primarily due to higher promotional activity at Athleta.”
  • Ike Boruchow, Wells Fargo, sought gross margin clarification. O’Connell explained, “Gross margin in Q2 would be similar to our first quarter gross margin… margins would be somewhat flat to leveraging slightly without the credit card impact.”
  • Simeon Siegel, BMO Capital Markets, asked about marketing spend. Dickson said, “We actually are spending less marketing dollars, but we're generating more revenue, we're generating more relevance.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a constructive but cautious tone, with repeated focus on tariffs, pricing strategies, and brand consistency. Inquiries about mitigation, pricing, and marketing efficiency signaled some skepticism about durability of recent gains.
  • Management’s tone remained confident, frequently referencing discipline and strategic rigor. Phrases like “we are committed” and “we remain focused” dominated both prepared remarks and responses, indicating ongoing confidence but with attention to potential volatility from tariffs.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment showed increased concern over tariffs, while management’s tone shifted toward more detailed discussion of mitigation strategies, maintaining an overall confident outlook.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for net sales and operating income growth remains unchanged at 1–2% and 8–10%, respectively, but this quarter included explicit quantification of the $100 million to $150 million net tariff impact, which was not as prominent or detailed last quarter.
  • Comp sales at Gap and Old Navy were lower than the previous quarter’s 7% and 3% (now 5% and 3%), with Banana Republic down from a 4% increase to flat, and Athleta’s decline widening from -2% to -8%.
  • Management’s focus shifted from celebrating brand reinvigoration to addressing tariff mitigation and supply chain diversification.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on consumer trends, channel dynamics, and cost management, but this quarter saw more persistent questions about tariffs and pricing power.
  • Management’s tone remains confident but now includes more nuance regarding external risks and mitigation measures.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariffs represent the most significant near-term risk, with management estimating a net operating income impact of $100 million to $150 million if current policy persists.
  • Uncertainty related to global economic and geopolitical conditions was highlighted as a factor in a “highly dynamic environment.”
  • Management outlined mitigation strategies—sourcing diversification, manufacturing adjustments, and inventory flexibility—while reaffirming their commitment to long-term brand health rather than short-term fixes.
  • Analysts repeatedly questioned the sufficiency and timing of these mitigation strategies and potential impacts on pricing and margins.

Final Takeaway

The Gap, Inc. leadership signaled continued confidence in executing its brand reinvigoration strategy, reaffirming guidance for modest sales and operating income growth in 2025 while proactively addressing the evolving tariff landscape. The company articulated specific mitigation plans expected to offset more than half of the potential tariff burden, and emphasized that pricing impacts to consumers are not anticipated at this time. Market share gains at Old Navy and Gap, strong cash reserves, and disciplined expense management reinforce the financial foundation, but management acknowledged that volatility from trade policy and ongoing efforts to reset Athleta and Banana Republic will continue to shape performance through the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Microsoft drives up Copilot AI adoption, lands big deal with Barclays: report
data.description:
Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) has apparently gained ground with its Copilot artificial intelligence assistants, landing major deals with Barclays (NYSE:BCS), among others, according to Bloomberg.

Judson Althoff, Microsoft's chief commercial officer, told employees during a company-wide town hall meeting today, Barclays intends to purchase 100,000 licenses for Copilot AI assistants, the report said, citing sources familiar with the event. Althoff also named Accenture (ACN), Toyota (TM) and Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY) as large-scale adopters of the AI assistants.

Microsoft has embedded Copilot, its flagship AI offering, across its products. During Microsoft's third quarter earnings call on April 30, CEO Satya Nadella listed an array Copilot-infused applications and strong adoptions.

"And we are previewing a first-of-its-kind SWE agent capable of asynchronously executing developer tasks," Nadella said. "All up, we now have over 15 million GitHub Copilot users up over 4x year-over-year, and both digital natives like Twilio (TWLO) and enterprises like Cisco (CSCO), HPE (HPE), Skyscanner and Target (TGT) continue to choose GitHub Copilot to equip their developers with AI throughout the entire dev life cycle."

Despite the positive rhetoric around Copilot, the report noted that Microsoft "hasn't disclosed a total customer count or the financial impact of those sales."

data.title: Sempra's Port Arthur Phase 2 approved to export LNG
data.description:
Sempra (NYSE:SRE) said Thursday the U.S. Department of Energy issued a permit allowing its Port Arthur LNG Phase 2 development project in Texas to export of up to ~13.5M metric tons/year of liquefied natural gas to markets in Europe and Asia.

The Phase 2 project is expected to include two liquefaction trains capable of producing ~13M metric tons/year of LNG, which would increase the total liquefaction capacity of the Port Arthur LNG facility from 13M tons/year for Phase 1, which is currently under construction, to as much as 26M tons/year.

Port Arthur LNG Phase 2, which still needs a final investment decision, is expected to export 1.91B cf/day once completed; Phase 1 is expected to begin exporting LNG in 2027.

Phase 2 is the first final LNG export approval under President Trump, who reversed the Biden administration's pause on project approvals.

data.title: Gold futures gain, reversing selloff sparked by court ruling on tariffs
data.description:
Gold futures closed higher Thursday, reversing losses early in the session following reports that the U.S. Court of International Trade deemed most of President Trump's tariffs illegal.

The news initially raised risk appetite and triggered a selloff in gold, but the risk-on sentiment was blunted as the Trump administration appealed the decision, then gold benefitted from buying interest from the dip in price, analysts said.

The assumption that the decision would be challenged, likely elevating it to the Supreme Court, replaced the risk-on sentiment with "uncertainty and confusion - that is safe-haven gold's wheelhouse," Robert Yawger of Mizuho Securities said in a note.

Gold also was aided by softer jobs data that showed the number of Americans filing new applications for unemployment benefits rose more than expected last week; if the data is a harbinger of a weakening labor market, the Federal Reserve could cut interest rates more quickly.

Bullion is considered a hedge against uncertainty and inflation, and it tends to perform well in low interest rate environments, since the metal does not generate interest.

The market also will scrutinize Friday's release of U.S. Personal Consumption Expenditures, one of the Fed's favored data points.

Front-mnth Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for June closed +0.7% to $3,317.10/oz, and front-month June silver (XAGUSD:CUR) finished +0.8% to $33.283/oz, snapping back-to-back daily losses for both metals.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Red Robin Gourmet Burgers swings to a profit launching shares 55% higher after-hours
data.description:
Red Robin Gourmet Burgers’ (NASDAQ:RRGB) ambitious plan to turn the business around continues to impress investors as the restaurant chain swung to a profit in the first quarter, doubled adjusted EBITDA from last year, lowered labor costs, and cut its debt by nearly $18M.

The results overshadowed lowered guidance for FY25 and expectations for comparable restaurant revenue to decline by 3%, launching shares more than 55% higher in Thursday’s after-hours trading.

“There is still more work to be done as we continue the comeback journey of this beloved brand and capture the significant opportunity in front of us," CEO David Pace said in a statement.

Total sales for the quarter increased by just 1% to an above-consensus $392.35M. But thanks to a 6.8% increase in net menu pricing, as well as lower labor costs and general expenses, Red Robin (NASDAQ:RRGB) swung to an adjusted profit of $0.20 per share versus a loss of $0.73 in the same quarter last year, beating expectations by $0.69. In addition, adjusted EBITDA improved to $27.9M from $13.4M a year ago.

Expectations for the current quarter were lowered to include a headwind of ~240 basis points due to a 2024 benefit from the change in the loyalty program not recurring in 2025. The company now expects adjusted EBITDA of $13M to $16M.

For FY25 total revenue was lowered to $1.21B to $1.23B from $1.225 to $1.250B previously, versus $1.23B estimates. Other estimates remain unchanged including restaurant level operating profit of 12% to 13%, adjusted EBITDA of $60M to $65M, and capital expenditures of ~$30M.

data.title: LanzaTech appoints Sushmita Koyanagi as CFO
data.description:
• LanzaTech Global (NASDAQ:LNZA) announced on Thursday certain transitions in its executive leadership team in connection with its recent financing and ongoing strategic measures. 
  • The announced leadership changes include:Sushmita Koyanagi appointed Chief Financial Officer, effective June 2, 2025. Amanda Fuisz to assume the role of interim General Counsel, effective June 13, 2025, and Gary Rieschel, a long-time serving Board member, to retire from the board of directors. 
  • Koyanagi succeeds Justin Pugh, who has been serving as LanzaTech’s interim CFO since January 2025 and who will maintain an advisory role with the company to assist in his transition and to provide other related support until June 30, 2025. 
  • The announced leadership changes and role consolidations are anticipated to result in annual cost reductions of approximately $1 million.

data.title: Laser Photonics faces Nasdaq non-compliance; shares down
data.description:
• Laser Photonics (NASDAQ:LASE) received a Nasdaq notice for failing to file its Q1 2025 and 2024 annual reports, both delayed due to its November 2024 asset acquisition from CMS.
  • The company has until June 16, 2025, to submit a compliance plan, with a potential extension through October 13, 2025.
  • LPC is working with auditors to resolve issues tied to CMS’s historical revenue practices and asset valuation.
  • LASE shares fall over 8% post-market on Thursday.
  • Source: Press release
